body_txt: Heroes sometimes lose. It's the general rule of fiction to the point of being a near Omnipresent Trope , with a few exceptions . A loss provides the hero a chance to learn valuable lessons like humility and resilience, an opportunity for Character Development , and keeps the villains fresh and threatening . After all, losing in Acts 1 and 2 doesn't mean a hero won't beat the villain in Act 3 . Then there are these heroes . These heroes never win. Ever . A Failure Hero might make some headway against a rival or villain in the first or second round, but the rival or villain neatly trounces them before the end credits, sometimes thanks to a Diabolus ex Machina . (The latter is especially common in potentially series-resolving situations .) Any attempt of his to rescue a Damsel in Distress will result in an embarrassing failure or result in being too late to save her . Any "wins" he does pull off are ambiguous and open-ended, further the villain's plan , or blatantly make things that much worse for the unwitting hero . This, of course, tends to rob a given episode or movie franchise of dramatic punch when the viewer's reaction to a hero making steps to resolving their lifelong goal is " You're Just Yanking Our Chains! " And if the hero is too much of a failure, the audience may decide to abandon the story in frustration . Behind this is usually the idea that the hero sucks that much. Plus he's the hero; the side of good is in an eternal struggle with evil ! This is especially common in comedies where the hero is a failure of some kind or where advancing his goals would end the show . More seriously, this is the typical fate of a Tragic Hero as their Fatal Flaw destroys any good they might accomplish. The two are not identical — a Tragic Hero may have major, genuine victories that are not invalidated by their later fall, and a Failure Hero may be the victim of bad luck and / or enemy action rather than their own weaknesses and bad decisions — but the crossover is significant. The loser counterpart of Invincible Hero . Not as prevalent as its sibling, but still common enough to warrant its own trope. The Small Steps Hero will become this in a truly Crapsack World . Please note that being saddled with the pesky Status Quo Is God or Failure Is the Only Option tropes isn't enough to qualify someone as a Failure Hero; they must fail at not only the thing the show says they can't succeed at but at nearly everything else due to continual plot contrivances . This trope is not for arcs where the hero(es) lose a lot, if they've won before or after. If the heroes lose for most of a series and then ultimately win or accomplish their goals at the end, it is ALSO not this trope. Compare Useless Protagonist , Heroic Wannabe , Super Loser , and Super Zeroes , the latter being the comedic version. Contrast Invincible Hero . Usually Not Badass Enough for Fans , and may lead to the utterance of the Eight Deadly Words or Too Bleak, Stopped Caring . Also see Ineffectual Sympathetic Villain , the evil counterpart.
